topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The cardboard box was a disaster. It was overflowing with crumpled papers, neatly stacked notebooks, and a truly staggering volume of homework from the last decade. I stared at the mountain of evidence on the kitchen table, my jaw tight. My mother, bless her oblivious heart, had apparently kept every single assignment from every school. I picked up a crumpled essay from sophomore year history, the ink faded, and felt a slow, hot burn rising in my chest. "How could you just… keep all of this?" I managed to choke out, the words sounding thin and sharp in the quiet room. The sheer audacity of it made my hands tremble slightly as I dropped the papers back into the box. This wasn't just clutter; this was a deliberate act of withholding, and it felt deeply disrespectful.

[story 2]

I found the stash tucked away in the attic, behind a stack of old photo albums. The air up there was thick with dust and the scent of aged paper. When I pulled out the first binder—a collection of meticulously graded math tests—a sudden, hot pressure built behind my eyes. I slammed the binder shut, the sound echoing loudly in the silence. "Seriously?" I muttered, my voice low and strained. I ran a hand through my hair, feeling the familiar tightness across my forehead. It wasn't just the volume; it was the implication. She had hoarded this entire history of my education, treating it like some kind of secret treasure trove. I felt a deep, frustrated surge, wanting to scream at the memory of her carelessness.

[story 3]

The sheer volume of completed work was overwhelming. I stood in the hallway, the box balanced precariously in my arms, and the weight of responsibility—or perhaps, the weight of her deception—pressed down on me. My knuckles were white where I gripped the cardboard edges. "This is absurd," I whispered, shaking my head slowly. I could see the years represented in those papers, each one a silent testament to effort she’d never acknowledged, never shared. A wave of intense annoyance washed over me, making my breathing shallow. I needed an explanation, a justification for this bizarre, monumental act of retention. I turned sharply toward the stairs, my steps heavy and deliberate, the need to confront whatever this situation represented burning brightly in my gut.
